texto: Safe in the sovereignty of the Godhead, we can count on the fact that WE, rather than just our friends, enemies, or circumstances, will be transformed.

texto: Help our response, Lord, not to be, "WHAT are You doing?!" Instead, give us the perspective to ask, "Lord, what would You teach?

texto: Make us, Lord, in Chesterton's distinction, travelers instead of tourists. Where the tourist defines his day by the expectation of seeing certain things, give us the traveler's openness to seeing what You will reveal.
explicacao: Citação de Brian Eshleman sobre temas ligados a fe. “Make us, Lord, in Chesterton's distinction, travelers instead of tourists. Where the tourist defi…” articula fe, curiosity, discipleship em registro de fe, com relevância para contextos como viagem e discipleship.

texto: What do we advertise about You by how we serve? Do we reflect the belief shown in Jesus’ parable that You are a hard master taking what isn’t Yours? Forgive us for even brief lapses into such churlishness. Or, do we reflect a joy in serving that radiates from an intimate and time-tested knowledge of the goodness of the One we serve? Paul and Daniel were confident of this sovereign goodness even when they were prisoners rather than courtiers, and we can likewise tap into a joy that defies circumstances. When this happens, the oft-disappointed world will notice and investigate.11/02/2010 blog
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Brian Eshleman que provoca passagem do tempo e prioridades. Ao questionar “What do we advertise about You by how we serve? Do we reflect the belief shown in Jesus’ parable …”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre sabedoria, calling, contentment, com foco em trabalho e contentment.

texto: Do we have Your Mark 10:39 faith that You will complete what You have begun in us? Or, do we look at our faltering desires and despair or excuse? Let us, Lord Jesus, hear Your Word over us more clearly and emphatically than we hear our own projections. Our predictions come from walking by sight, and they are SO often wrong that we ought to abandon them out of sheer practicality.
